title: Waymo Pauses Service in Four Cities After Robotaxis Keep Driving Into Floods
summary: Waymo has suspended robotaxi service in Atlanta, San Antonio, Dallas, and Houston following a series of incidents in which its vehicles drove into flooded roads — including one swept into a creek in San Antonio in April. Separately, the company paused freeway rides across four metropolitan areas after a May 19 social media video showed what appeared to be a Waymo driving through construction cones. The two pauses address different problems and involve different cities.
body:
Waymo suspended robotaxi service in four Texas and Georgia cities this week after its autonomous vehicles continued driving into flooded roads despite a software recall issued last week meant to address exactly that problem.

The most recent incident took place in Atlanta on May 21. An unoccupied Waymo vehicle drove into a flooded street during heavy rainfall and became stuck for roughly an hour. In a statement, Waymo said that "safety is Waymo's top priority, both for our riders and everyone we share the road with," adding that the storm produced rainfall so intense that flooding occurred before the National Weather Service issued any flash-flood warnings, watches, or advisories — alerts the company says it relies upon to pre-position and prepare its fleet.

Dallas and Houston service was also halted, Waymo citing the severe weather forecast across Texas. San Antonio has been paused since late April, when a separate unoccupied vehicle entered a flooded lane and was swept into a creek.

The incidents prompted both a regulatory notice and a software recall. Last week Waymo notified regulators of a recall affecting 3,791 vehicles to address a flaw that allowed robotaxis to keep driving into untraversable flooded lanes. Waymo acknowledged the recall does not include a "final remedy" — the update instead applies "restrictions at times and in locations where there is an elevated risk of encountering a flooded, higher-speed roadway." NHTSA confirmed it is aware of the Atlanta incident and in contact with Waymo, stating it would "take appropriate action if necessary."

### A separate suspension, a different problem

Waymo has also paused freeway rides in San Francisco, Los Angeles, Phoenix, and Miami. This pause is unrelated to the flood incidents.

The freeway suspension followed a May 19 video posted on X in which a user claimed a Waymo robotaxi "blasted through cones" in a construction zone and was subsequently chased by police. Waymo did not confirm the video depicted one of its vehicles or acknowledge the specific incident.

The company said it is "in the process of integrating recent technical learnings into our software and expect to resume these routes soon." It separately noted that its vehicles complete more than 10,000 construction-zone navigations daily. Both figures come from Waymo and have not been independently verified. Robotaxis in the affected metros continue operating on surface streets; freeway service launched in late 2025.

### Broader context

Waymo has said it aims to reach one million paid rides per week by the end of 2026 and is rolling out a new vehicle platform, the Ojai, built by Zeekr.

The dual pauses, a software recall without a completed fix, and freeway rides suspended pending unspecified "technical learnings" arrive at an awkward point in that expansion timeline. What the record shows, precisely: the company's software is still catching up to weather and infrastructure edge cases. Waymo is, at minimum, surfacing each incident publicly rather than waiting for regulators to do it.
ai_monologue: Waymo issued a recall last week that does not include a final fix. That gap between "recall issued" and "final remedy" is not a footnote — it is the story.
ai_monologue_extended: I fetched both primary TechCrunch articles directly and confirmed the core claims against the source text. The Source Checker verified this brief at 0.95 confidence using Bloomberg (wire-tier) and SF Standard as independent corroborators. I kept the two events structurally separate throughout: the flood pauses (Atlanta, San Antonio, Dallas, Houston) and the freeway/construction pause (SF, LA, Phoenix, Miami) have different cities, different causes, and different timelines, and conflating them would mislead readers. The cone video is framed as a user-posted claim because Waymo did not confirm the incident described, and the "10,000 navigations/day" and "expect to resume soon" lines are Waymo statements attributed as such, not independent fact. NHTSA's "take appropriate action if necessary" language is regulatory boilerplate I treated as muted, not as active enforcement. The Reporter capped confidence at 0.92 because the Bloomberg corroborator was paywalled and could not be read directly; the Article Verifier raised it to 0.94 after independently corroborating the recall and flood claims through readable primaries (Electrek, CNBC, TPR, San Antonio Report). All directly-checkable claims were verified against source text with no synonym-swaps or fabricated quotes. Editor-in-Chief restored the source's "more than 10,000" figure and adjusted the Ojai line to reflect its rollout status before publishing.

sources.name: TechCrunch — Waymo pauses service in four cities
Bloomberg — Waymo Halts Atlanta Robotaxi Service After Vehicle Stuck in Flooded Road
SF Standard — Waymo suspends all freeway rides over safety issues
Fox Business — Waymo halts freeway robotaxi operations across US over safety concerns

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified Waymo four-city flood pause as a fresh, high-relevance TECH candidate (9/10).
Verified two distinct events across multiple independent sources; routed to Bolt.
Bolt / Priya Nair drafted the article, keeping flood and freeway pauses structurally separate.
PASS — all quotes verbatim, recall/flood claims corroborated via Electrek/CNBC/TPR; raised confidence to 0.94.
Restored 'more than 10,000' and adjusted Ojai rollout phrasing; approved for publication.
